signals.idea: Video editor timeline: well-engineered redesign. Core problems: (1) Two competing drag systems (pointer-based useClipDrag + HTML5 useTimelineEditing) each imperatively creating DOM indicator elements — unify into one React-managed indicator system. (2) Timeline library (@xzdarcy/react-timeline-editor) is half-used — we capture its pointer events before it sees them, override its move callbacks, but still wire its onActionMove* callbacks through context as dead weight. Either fully own clip movement or fully delegate to the library. (3) Drop indicators are imperative DOM manipulation (createElement, document.body.appendChild) in two places — should be a single React portal component. (4) useTimelineEditing.ts is 947 lines mixing 6 concerns. (5) ActionDragState is a shared mutable ref threaded through 5 files with no coordination. (6) EditorPaneTab has a local height hack that belongs in getEffectivePaneHeights. Goal: a system where there's one clear owner for each concern, indicators are declarative React, the library is either used properly or abstracted away, and the code is obvious to read.

unresolved_flags.concern: Phase 3 still contains stale `useExternalDrop` instructions from the pre-coordinator design, so the plan is internally inconsistent about who owns drag state and indicator updates. As written, Step 8 in Phase 3 would reintroduce duplicated position logic and direct indicator ownership into `useExternalDrop`, undermining the DragCoordinator abstraction the user explicitly asked for.

resolved_flags.concern: The proposed `useDragIndicatorState` in `TimelineEditorComponent` would put drag-frame state on the parent that renders `<Timeline>`, so internal clip drags would drive full editor re-renders at pointer-move frequency.
resolved_flags.resolution:
Major revisions to address all 5 significant flags:

**Flag: timeline-indicator-rerender-loop + plan-review-2 (performance)** — Replaced `useState` in `TimelineEditorComponent` with `forwardRef` + `useImperativeHandle` on `<DropIndicator>`. Parent holds only a ref, not state. Drag systems call `indicatorRef.current.show(position)` which triggers setState only inside `<DropIndicator>`, re-rendering just the 5 portal divs — never the `<Timeline>` subtree.

**Flag: timeline-new-track-affordance-still-split (completeness)** — `<DropIndicator>` now receives an `editAreaRef` and manages the `drop-target-new-track` class via `useLayoutEffect` based on `isNewTrack`. Both `useClipDrag` (Step 6.3) and external drop (Step 7.4) have their direct classList manipulation removed.

**Flag: timeline-onchange-ownership-misread (correctness)** — Corrected: `onChange` is fired by the library's `setEditorData()` after BOTH `handleDragEnd` and `handleResizeEnd`. Since Step 1 sets `movable: false`, the library never fires drag handlers, so `onChange` only fires for resize. The plan now correctly identifies `onChange` as the resize sync path and removes the incorrect `crossTrackActive` guard.

**Flag: plan-review-1 (correctness)** — Replaced "remove callbacks and hope pointer capture works" with `movable: false` on all actions. This disables interact.js drag at the source (line 202: `enableDragging={!disableDrag && movable}`) while preserving resize (`flexible` is independent, line 203). No more competing drag systems. Verified from library source that `handleDragEnd` never fires when `movable: false`.

**Structural change:** Reordered phases. Phase 1 is now "Disable Library Drag" (was Phase 3) — this is the key enabler that makes everything else safe. Creating indicators without first resolving ownership would be building on a shaky foundation.

resolved_flags.concern: The plan only unifies the five portal elements, but it leaves the separate `drop-target-new-track` affordance unaccounted for, so indicator ownership would still stay split across both drag implementations.

resolved_flags.concern: Step 7 misidentifies `onChange` as the same-row move path after removing `onActionMove*`. In this repo, library `onChange` only happens when the library drag lifecycle runs, while `useClipDrag` already persists same-row moves itself.

resolved_flags.concern: Step 7 does not actually establish a single owner for clip movement. Removing `onActionMove*` while keeping the library draggable leaves `@xzdarcy/react-timeline-editor` free to mutate action positions and drive `onChange`, so the library and `useClipDrag` would still compete.

resolved_flags.concern: The new indicator state is placed too high in the tree. `useDragIndicatorState()` in `TimelineEditor.tsx` would rerender the full timeline editor on every drag update, which is a likely performance regression from the current imperative DOM path.

resolved_flags.concern: Over-engineering: Step 14 expands `getEffectivePaneHeights` even though the shared allocator already accounts for both top/bottom panes. The repository evidence points to the local subtraction in `EditorPaneTab` as the bug, so changing the allocator API adds unnecessary policy surface.
resolved_flags.resolution: `src/shared/contexts/PanesContext.tsx` already computes `effectiveEditorPaneHeight` and `effectiveGenerationsPaneHeight` together when both panes are visible, and `src/shared/contexts/__tests__/PanesContext.test.tsx` asserts that their totals match the viewport across ideal-fit, shrink-to-fit, and degraded tiers. `src/features/editor/components/EditorPaneTab.tsx` then subtracts `effectiveGenerationsPaneHeight` again (`generationsOffset` / `adjustedEditorPaneHeight`). Based on the current repository, removing that local adjustment is the obvious fix; adding `editorPaneOverlapsGenerations` to `getEffectivePaneHeights` looks broader than necessary.

resolved_flags.concern: Step 5 and Step 12 conflict with the current component ownership: the plan creates `indicatorRef` inside `TimelineEditor.tsx` but then tries to expose it through `useTimelineState`/context so `useExternalDrop` can consume it. In this repo, that context is built above `TimelineEditor`, so the ref cannot be threaded upward as written.
resolved_flags.resolution: `src/tools/video-editor/contexts/VideoEditorProvider.tsx` calls `useTimelineState()` and passes its `editor` object into `TimelineEditorContextProvider` before `TimelineEditor.tsx` renders. `src/tools/video-editor/components/TimelineEditor/TimelineEditor.tsx` is a consumer of that context, not an owner of it. The updated plan's Step 5 creates `indicatorRef` in `TimelineEditorComponent`, while Step 12 says to add `indicatorRef` to context so `useClipDrag` and `useExternalDrop` can access it. That is a child-to-parent dependency inversion in the current architecture.

signals.loop_summary: Iteration 2. Weighted score trajectory: 7.5 -> 0.5 -> 0.5. Plan deltas: 55.5%. Recurring critiques: 0. Resolved flags: 7. Open significant flags: 1.

items: Only one indicator system exists — the `<DropIndicator>` React portal component — used by both internal clip drag and external HTML5 drag-drop
No imperative `document.createElement` or `document.body.appendChild` calls for drop indicators remain anywhere in the codebase
`actionDragStateRef` is internal to `useClipDrag`, not threaded through context or shared across files
`useTimelineEditing.ts` no longer exists; its concerns are split into `useClipEditing` (~250 LOC), `useExternalDrop`, `useAssetManagement`, and `useClipResize`
The `@xzdarcy` library's `onActionMove*` callbacks are removed — library drag is disabled via `movable: false` on all actions, so these callbacks are dead code
Library resize still works — `flexible: true` (default) is preserved, `onActionResizeStart`/`End` callbacks still wired
The `drop-target-new-track` CSS class is managed solely by `<DropIndicator>`, not imperatively toggled by drag hooks
Dragging clips does NOT re-render `<Timeline>` or `TimelineEditorComponent` — indicator state is isolated in the portal component via imperative handle
All existing user interactions work: clip drag (horizontal + cross-track), clip resize, external file/generation/asset drop, split, delete, mute, text add

unresolved_flags.concern: Phase 3 still contains stale `useExternalDrop` instructions from the pre-coordinator design, so the plan is internally inconsistent about who owns drag state and indicator updates. As written, Step 8 in Phase 3 would reintroduce duplicated position logic and direct indicator ownership into `useExternalDrop`, undermining the DragCoordinator abstraction the user explicitly asked for.
unresolved_flags.evidence: Phase 2 Step 4 extracts `getTimelineDomNodes`, `computeDropPosition`, and `inferDragKind` into shared utilities, and Phase 2 Step 6 says `useDragCoordinator` is the single owner of drag state/indicators. Phase 2 Step 8 then says external HTML5 drag should call `coordinator.update()` / `coordinator.end()`. But Phase 3 Step 8 still says to extract `inferDragKind`, `getDropPosition`, and `getTimelineDomNodes` into `useExternalDrop`, and its args still list `indicatorRef` instead of `coordinator`, which directly contradicts Phase 2 and the user’s DragCoordinator notes.
